response: Programming to an interface is a strategic choice driven by the need for flexibility rather than a complete understanding of every future relationship. When architects begin with interfaces, they often anticipate a **lack** of concrete implementation details that will only emerge later in the development cycle. This approach allows different teams to work on distinct modules without needing to know the specific **path** of inheritance or the internal logic of other components. Unlike abstract classes, which enforce a rigid hierarchy, interfaces enable a many-to-many relationship where unrelated objects can share behavior without sharing a common ancestor. Consider a **file** system where a text processor and a video encoder both implement a `Readable` interface; they share the ability to be read, yet their internal structures remain entirely distinct. If you were to force them into an abstract class, you would create unnecessary coupling and limit the ability to mix and match behaviors dynamically. The **salad** of diverse components in a modern application relies on this loose coupling to remain maintainable and scalable. By defining contracts early, architects ensure that the system can evolve without breaking existing code, even when the final relationships between objects are not yet fully mapped. This foresight prevents the architectural rigidity that often plagues systems built solely on inheritance.
